cards.definition: The full set of dimensions — perceptions, associations, expectations, and experiences — that differentiate a product in customers' minds beyond its physical or functional attributes.
The NPD stage in which multiple product concepts are created and explored before one is selected for further development.
The strategic act of differentiating a product through branding so that it competes on dimensions other than price alone, reducing substitutability.
Customer needs that are readily articulated and are typically already being addressed by existing market offerings.
The technical dimension of the Three Innovation Challenge — whether the organization has or can develop the capability to build and deliver the product.
A customer insight framework holding that customers "hire" products to accomplish a job, framed as "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome]."
Needs that customers may not be aware of or cannot yet express; addressing them can produce products that dramatically exceed expectations.
A clear, concise written expression of a customer need — written in "what" not "how," specific, positive, active voice, using "can" not "must/should," neutral and understandable.
The end-to-end sequence of organizational activities — from opportunity identification through launch — used to conceive, design, and commercialize a product.
The first stage of the NPD process, in which the organization identifies and selects among potential market needs or technology opportunities to pursue.

A portfolio risk-management approach in which a large initial pool of opportunities is progressively narrowed through multiple evidence-based filters until only the most promising opportunities remain.
A macro-environmental scanning framework covering Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal factors affecting an organization's general environment.

A competitive analysis framework examining Threat of New Entrants, Bargaining Power of Buyers, Bargaining Power of Suppliers, Threat of Substitutes, and Intensity of Rivalry in an operating environment.
Any offering — physical good, service, or other market offering — that can satisfy a want or need and is offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption.
Quantified product requirements expressed as a metric, a target value, and units, derived from customer needs statements and prioritized by importance.
An opportunity evaluation framework testing whether a market opportunity is real, winnable, and worth it — mapping to desirability, competitive feasibility, and business viability respectively.
The requirement that a successful product simultaneously be desirable to people, viable for the business, and technically feasible — failure on any single dimension disqualifies the opportunity.
Needs that customers can easily express but that are not yet broadly addressed by available market offerings — representing a clear, validated opportunity space.
The business dimension of the Three Innovation Challenge — whether the product can generate sufficient revenue, margin, or strategic value to justify investment.
